posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Have landed in Paris after warming up in Amsterdam for four days. Colin thought the best place to warm up for Paris is in Paris and has had a week’s head start on us.

We generally choose one place for a blow-out meal in Paris and Le Grand Vefour was it this year. Colin felt right at home as we entered the restaurant with all its Belle ’Epoqueness’ and there was enough Ramonet on the list to keep him happy. Rather than forsake a bottle of Ramonet by taking an aperitif we dove straight into a 2013 Domaine Ramonet Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru ‘Morgeot’ Blanc. It was immediately generous with orchard fruit aromas and flavours, some spearmint and plenty of underlying minerality. It had Morgeot size coupled with Ramonet elegance and is a wine of good detail and finesse. It was good with an amuse of tuna with squid ink and avocado and even better with ravioli of foie with black truffle.

When presented with the luncheon menu the page is open to the a la carte section. Turn back a page and one of Paris’ absolute bargains presents itself. The lunch menu here allows a selection of an entrée, main course and dessert. You also get a couple of amuse, a cheese course and enough pre, post and parallel desserts to satisfy Augustus Gloop.

Our menu proper started with a lobster soup for the girls, that Colin suggested may have had at least 36 lobsters in the reduction. It was so intense. There was some ginger, coconut and coriander giving complexity and it was triumphant with the Chassagne. The boys all went for Foie gras de canard en terrine. It was super rich and gamey. Finely diced apple was soused in something exotic and spicy and there were a few dots of a parsley emulsion on the plate that were perhaps more decorative than integral to the dish.

The sommelier here is a good guy and intuitive. I started making noises that perhaps we’ll get a bit of a Ramonet vertical going and have a look at the ’12 Morgeot. He said ‘I have a 2010 ‘Ruchottes’ that’s not on the list, would you like that at the same price’. Not wanting to offend I quickly accepted his offer. The 2010 Domaine Ramonet Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru ‘Les Ruchottes’ is a rich, textural, pulpy wine of great intensity and presence. It has great power and shape and is crammed with orchard fruits. There’s a suggestion of apricot and plenty of mineral just below the flesh. It has Grand Cru size and complexity and was splendid with Turbot à la meunière. The fish could have been taken off the grill 20 seconds earlier but was still very tasty and came with an interesting mélange of red cabbage things including a foam and polenta made from said cabbage. Lily, Patrick and Heidi absolutely hammered their duck. A perfectly cooked duck breast came with an accompaniment of mushrooms and Jerusalem artichoke. There were plenty of orange and carrot flavoured things on the plate that worked well with the duck. We all got a little bowl of the creamiest mashed potato with veal jus and black truffle. One of life’s great things.

Heidi and I still had a bit of white left in our glass as the cheese arrived. Colin didn’t. I was on one of my long rants about how white wine really is the only option with cheese and red wine isn’t. Colin interjected and let us know that he’d just have to plough on with his glass of red. The 2012 Domaine Mugneret-Gibourg Vosne-Romanée is really a lovely drink. Plush, velvety fruit, floral spice and decent depth and volume for its level. The cheeses here are perfect. 48-month-old Comté is something to behold. Epoisse is at its gooiest best and Patrick’s ewe’s milk cheese from Corsica was indeed the best Corsican cheese made from ewe’s milk that we have encountered.

Just as desserts were about to come out Patrick got his hairy banana out and started to wave it around, much to the delight of the waiting staff. Desserts are splendid here. Apple is roasted in cider. It comes with a delicious Granny Smith apple and lime sorbet. The chocolate dessert is kind of like a Wagon Wheel that has been given the posh patisserie treatment. The exterior of chocolate has more shine than a first over Kookaburra and tempered chocolate to the side is piped with the most wonderful mousse.

After a coffee we took the hairy banana around the restaurant and let him sit at the seat where General Bonaparte used to dine. I think the staff were quite relieved when we eventually departed.

Have had a few outstanding Rhone wines over the past week.

2012 Thierry Allemand Cornas Reynard: Quite simply the most pure and perfumed expression of Syrah one could hope for. It has delicious black cherry and plum fruit. There are some notes of menthol, grilled meats and black pepper. The palate is silky yet there’s terrific drive and presence. The wine did shut down in the decanter a little but provided much drinking pleasure.

2011 Thierry Allemand Cornas Reynard: It is not as pure as the ’12 and has a bit more wildness and funk. There are aromas of compost, game and blue fruits. It has a light herbal quality and the fruit intensifies at its heart with air. There are notes of liqueur cherry and menthol and length is good.

2015 Pierre Gonon St. Joseph: Smells like a footy change room at half time, all Dencorub, meat and sweat. With air there’s ripe blood plum fruit. It has plenty of floral nuance and a saline mineral quality. There are punchy florals and the wine is full and relatively low acid but lacks no freshness.

2015 Domaine Gramenon Côtes du Rhône Cuvée A Pascal S.: An absolute beauty. It’s kind of like Cru Bojo with a bit more heft. There are black cherry and raspberry fruits. It has a suggestion of violets on the nose. It smells sweet but has plenty of savoury flavour and is a wine that is just so juicy and fleshy that you have to drink it fast.

A couple of beautiful bottles from Ghislaine Barthod and an excellent meal at Le Severo. The steak Hachette is killer and the côte de bœuf may be the best in Paris. Given that I’ve only had côte de bœuf in about 8 Parisian restaurants you can take my comment with a grain of fleur de sel if you like.

2013 Domaine Ghislaine Barthod Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Cras: Discreet, cool nose of rocks, meat, red and black fruits and violets. It is detailed and chiseled in the mouth. You feel every rocky bump and there’s enough flesh from the sweet fruit to allow you to take a peek now without getting hurt. Needs time and there’s certainly plenty of potential here.

2010 Domaine Ghislaine Barthod Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Cras: Quite an engaging nose of rose petal, smoke and black cherries. The palate has real sap and drive and a stony mineral base. It is deep and penetrating and the finish fans out. Still very youthful but thoroughly enjoyable.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
We first dined at L’Ourcine over a decade ago when the sous chef left Regalade to set up his own place. Not sure if it’s the same bloke behind the pots and pans but the food is bloody good. Cousin Lucy had just hit Paris, Lily was on the mend, Colin was in grand form so we all headed here for a leisurely Saturday lunch.

The wine list is not so good. We started with a 2011 Macon that hipster Somms of the world orgasm over. It was terrible. Colin thought it was an affront to bring such a thing to the table, citing it was like serving food riddled with salmonella. Lucy suggested it should have been drunk in 2010. I was much politer and drank it holding my nose. Our two reds for the day were much better. Our 2015 Marcel Lapierre Morgon was delicious. It had ripe red and black fruits. It was quite elegant smelling of fresh violets and lavender. There was a gentle meaty/muskiness to the wine. The palate was silky and tannins sweet and round. A 2015 Domaine Gilles Barge St. Joseph Clos Martinets was marked by its meaty reduction. It breathed up and had a core of black fruits. There was some dried herbs and liniment and a finish that was very savoury with baked earth flavours lingering once the wine was swallowed.

Food commenced with an excellent amuse of leek soup. Entrée for me was a superb mussel broth en croûte. Once you broke into the lid and stirred in pastry with broth, vegetables and mussels you ended up with a mélange of loveliness. Lucy and Lily’s chicken salad was served cold with fresh Spring vegetables. Tarragon was liberally strewn throughout and it was a dish of great vitality. Colin didn’t share his ravioli of spider crab. Must have been good. Heidi’s rock fish velouté burst with flavour.

Main course of rabbit for a couple of us was outstanding. Patrick has a sticky chop of pork belly that nearly glued his lips together. It didn’t quite as later during the meal he slid under the table and was making special comments during our conversation. Lucy’s onglet de veau with a shallot sauce was a classic partisan bistro dish, executed with great aplomb. Colin also didn’t share his scallops but was making noises of content.

For dessert, my pot de crème of chocolate and espresso flavours was beautiful. Lucy had one of the great desserts I’ve encountered. A sable with crème fraiche between the biscuits that had some poached pear studded through. Just delightful. Patrick had a quenelle of chocolate mousse with a quenelle of vanilla cream. Coffee is worth taking if you simply require more caffeine in your system.

Like La Regalade this is a proper French bistro not without its faults but well worth a visit.

The Japanese fusion scene is well and truly alive in Paris and we had splendid meals at Alliance and L’Archeste. Burgundy suits the food so well.

2011 Marc Colin et Fils Saint-Aubin 1er Cru En Remilly: Subtle and delicate with whiffs of white flowers, chalk, toast and citrus. It is compact and linear in the mouth but lacks no richness. There’s excellent rocky detail and outstanding cut.

2003 Comte Armand Pommard 1er Cru Clos des Epeneaux: Kind of like a gateway wine between Bordeaux and Burg drinkers. It has plenty of leather and meat. The fruit is dark, brooding, sweet and intense. It is ripe but not overblown and there’s great intensity in the mouth. It has plenty of grip and chew and it is imposing without being over the top.

posts.cooked:
Pot d’Etain is about the right distance between Paris and Meursault to stop for a leisurely lunch and raid a few gems off one of the great wine lists of the world. The last time we did so, Colin got held up, due to circumstances beyond his control, and arrived 2 hours late. This time we all had money on the fact that he’d be there waiting for us when we got there, as you can generally set your watch by his on timeliness. He rang just as we arrived to apologise that he’d be half an hour late. I think the circumstances may have been more within his control this time.

Arriving half an hour late was a stroke of genius on his behalf. He skipped all of the fluffing around that one does at the start to oraganise a wine and we even chose what he was going to eat. He arrived just as a bottle of Coche and a delicious lobster salad hit the table. The 2011 Coche-Dury Meursault 1er Cru Caillerets is very youthful. It has some petrol and sappy orchard fruits. It is rich and unctuous in the mouth with Grand Cru weight and intensity. There’s real chalky structure and it has serious chew on the back-end. Brilliant but needs a decade in the cellar.

For main course we had 7 hour cooked lamb that required red wine. A bottle of 2012 Domaine Armand Rousseau Père et Fils Gevrey-Chambertin 1er Cru Clos St. Jacques was utterly sublime. Young but with exquisite balance and proportion. There are notes of cranberry, black cherry, violets, black earth and ginger. It is full, rich and creamy with great intensity yet it is light on its feet. Tannins are spherical and length superb.

I took my own advice regarding cheese and moved back to white wine to go with a splendid wedge of epoisse and some aged Comté. A 2010 Bernard Boisson-Vadot Meursault 1er Cru Les Genevrières was just the ticket. Classic Meursault with its slightly exotic fruit profile and rich and heady aromas and flavours. There’s some white flower action and a touch of apricot. It has a fruit-sweet heart and is cut by lively, minerally acidity.

This is a restaurant that is worth a detour but be warned, you cannot buy everything that is referenced on the list. There are super rare Roumier Musignys and Coche Corton-Charlemagnes that the owner sells at his discretion to whom he wants. You will not struggle finding something else to drink mind you.

Lucy roasted a wonderful chicken from the Meursault Boucherie last night. Opened a few nice botts.

N.V. Jacques Selosse Champagne Rosé: Disgorged 11th May, 2016. Complex and ethereal with notes of smoked meats, red berries, nuts and chalk. Dense, rich and powerful but not heavy. Great acid spine and laden with chalky dry extract on the finish.

2011 Domaine Follin-Arbelet Corton-Charlemagne: Cool, discreet nose of Arum Lily, mineral, slate and white peach. There’s compact orchard fruits in the mouth. It is linear and long with a steely spine. Classic Corton.

1963 Maison Leroy Beaune: Wonderful aged Burgundy, drinking at the top of its game. It is engaging, ethereal and highly perfumed. There are notes of strawberries, black earth, pine needle sap and meat. It is fine and lacy in the mouth. It is not particularly deep or long but draws you in with each sniff and sip and was such a treat to drink.

1999 Domaine Daniel Rion et Fils Echezeaux: Started of tight and unyielding but breathed up brilliantly in the glass. There was some black cherry, earth and beef stock on the nose. In the mouth it had a dense heart of liqueur cherry. There was plenty of savoury nuance and the wine really builds through the palate fanning out on the finish. It is still very youthful but has great perfume and balance.

posts.cooked:
Popped into Domaine Vincent Dancer late yesterday to taste a few ‘15’s with Vincent.

2015 Vincent Dancer Bourgogne Blanc: Fruit from around Puligny and Meursault. It is ripe with some white flower action. It has good richness and some citrus tang. Really good for its level.

2015 Vincent Dancer Chassagne-Montrachet: Off vines planted in 1999. The nose is all pure white peach. It has good shape with a fruit sweet heart. There’s some nice aniseed spice and it is a generous wine that has good freshness.

2015 Vincent Dancer Meursault Les Corbins: It could only be Meursault, unless of course you guess it’s from somewhere else. An exotic fruit profile, great richness and shapely. There’s a hint of toast and a finish that is sappy and long.

2015 Vincent Dancer Meursault Les Grands Charrons: More mineral than the Corbins. You get almond butter coupled with ripe orchard fruits and some fennel. It fills the mouth with flavour and finishes with good energy.

2015 Vincent Dancer Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru La Romanée: Expressive nose of toast, floral spice and ripe peach. It is full, sappy, dense and intense. The balance is such that its delicious now but it should age well.

2015 Vincent Dancer Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Tete du Clos: This is off a vineyard planted in 1954. Complex aromatics of green melon, white peach and wet stones. It is cool, with rocky detail. There’s ripeness and sap and everything is in perfect balance and proportion. There’s plenty of dry extract to the finish.

2015 Vincent Dancer Meursault 1er Cru Les Perrières: There’s a minerally/steely base upon which ripe peach and citrus fruits play. It is complex with great breadth and intensity. Terrific wine.

2015 Vincent Dancer Chevalier-Montrachet: Vines planted in 1952. Finesse coupled with precision. Powerful and textural but with awesome restraint. It is layered and haves a bit of everything but not too much of anything. Superb.

Vincent offered a sneak peek at a couple of ’16 reds from barrel.

2016 Vincent Dancer Bourgogne Vieilles Vignes: Bright and perfumed with raspberry and cherry fruits. Plenty of potential.

2016 Vincent Dancer Pommard 1er Cru Les Pèzerolles: Has Pommard power and muscle. There are notes of menthol, sour cherry, rhubarb and wild herbs. Plenty of wine here.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Jean-Claude Boisset winemaker Gregory Patriat is at the top of his game. He has a new winery that will be ready for the 2017 harvest and he has nearly finished bottling a fabulous suite of 2015 wines. There are a couple of new vineyards that he managed to get some fruit off in ’15 and his wines in both colours have the depth and concentration of the vintage but maintain outstanding freshness.

2015 Jean-Claude Boisset St. Véran Les Pommards: This bursts with yellow fruits. It is rich and intense of flavour with plenty of mineral and touch of spice.

2015 Jean-Claude Boisset Marsannay Blanc: There’s plenty happening on the nose, honeysuckle, aniseed, peach and flint. It has good shape, texture and volume. The finish is punchy and energetic.

2015 Jean-Claude Boisset Saint-Aubin 1er Cru En Remilly: This is tight, bright and linear. There’s dense, sappy white peach fruit. It is a wine of excellent purity and tension. There’s plenty of saline minerality and great length.

2015 Jean-Claude Boisset Meursault Le Limozin: This is off a vineyard high on the Meursault slope with a vine age of around 90 years. It has such terrific energy and brightness coupled with the richness of the vintage. There’s intense citrus fruit and a suggestion of honeysuckle. It has weight and power without heaviness and is loaded with mineral.

2015 Jean-Claude Boisset Puligny-Montrachet Le Trézin: Quite closed for now. Tight and dense. Loaded with sappy orchard fruits. Very long.

2015 Jean-Claude Boisset Marsannay Rouge: This weighs in at just 12.7% alcohol but packs a flavour hit. Ripe plums and cherries are supported by well-judged spicy oak. It is rich, dense, chewy and long.

2015 Jean-Claude Boisset Maranges 1er Cru La Fussière: Gregory called this an atomic bomb. It explodes with flavour. Spicy dark fruits have concentrated, sweet Pinot Noir flavour. There’s good depth and loads of earth. Terrific value for money.
2015 Jean-Claude Boisset Chorey-les-Beaune Les Beaumonts: The vines here are some of the oldest in Burgundy, planted in 1902. It is a dense wine with plenty of old vine sap. The fruit profile is dark and you get some sarsaparilla and aniseed flavours. There’s a grating of fresh ginger and all aromas and flavours are back-lit by bright, minerally acidity.

2015 Jean-Claude Boisset Gevrey-Chambertin en Champs: The vines are around 90 years old here. It is quite elegant and refined yet there’s no shortage of power. Fruits are sweet and red and there’s a thread of spice. Structure is sinewy and the finish quite savoury.

2015 Jean-Claude Boisset Aloxe-Corton 1er Cru Les Valozières: Black fruits, dense and chunky. You feel the 50% whole bunch with some punchy floral lift and the grip it imparts on the back-end. It has plenty of earth and a touch of meat. There’s excellent depth and length of flavour is impressive.

2015 Jean-Claude Boisset Nuits St. Georges Coteaux du Bois: the first vintage from this vineyard for Gregory. It is situated high on the hill behind the Village and the wine is cool, minerally and tight. There are crunchy red berries, some rose petal and a fine lacy feel in the mouth. It bristles with energy on the finish.

2015 Jean-Claude Boisset Pommard 1er Cru Les Arvelets: This is fine and pretty Pommard with delicious sweet berry fruits. It has good posture with a minerally spine and excellent proportion and balance. It is 12.7% alcohol and has very good purity and intensity of fruit.

2015 Jean-Claude Boisset Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Charmes: Spicy and generous, laden with creamy black cherry fruit. Full, floral, complex and layered. Expansive in the mouth. Solid mineral base. So long. Quite brilliant.

2015 Jean-Claude Boisset Clos de la Roche: Sweet, dense, deep and penetrating. It’s all black fruits and licorice. You get the engaging scent of fresh violets as you swirl the wine in the glass. It has great power and breadth and is light on its feet. This is a wine of outstanding authority and finesse.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
I didn’t think Troisgros could be improved on. The 6 or 7 times we have been before we have had faultless experiences. Well it has been improved and its simply the matter of the location and dining room. Their new digs on a farm property outside of Roanne are splendid. I won’t even try to explain the architecture and interior design, but it is ‘weird arse’ and it works. The place has a sense of calm and tranquility and the luminescence is as bright as Michel Troisgros’ food.

Michel was on hand as we arrived and took us on a brief tour of the kitchen where the same sense of calm prevails. Patrick counted 12 chefs in there and then proceeded to pinch and prod Lily while we engaged in conversation with Michel and his son César. The zen-like calm had been broken and we got ushered back into the dining room proper.

We nibbled on a few excellent amuse while perusing the wine list. Three pieces of shortbread had preserved pumpkin flavoured with cassis, Szechuan pepper and curry. They got the gastric juices flowing immediately. The wine-list is a cracker and Coche seemed like a good place to start…… and finish. A 2011 Coche-Dury Meursault ‘Les Rougeots’ was drinking beautifully. It was rich and ripe, with almond butter and white peach notes. It was full in the mouth with a sappy feel and some gentle spice. It worked a treat with a dish of thin strips of dried carrot, shrimp, capers, garlic chips, fresh herbs and chili oil. What a dish of texture, elegant flavour, complexity and balance.

The kid’s menu is a beauty. When Heidi had a quick read through she spied the famous dish of Salmon and sorrel that Michel’s father used to make. She immediately said ‘right kids, you’re having this’. They sensed that this was one of those no negotiables and didn’t argue. We have never had it before and it deserves its cult status. A perfectly cooked piece of fish. Exquisite creamy sauce cut by the acidity of fresh sorrel. Yum. Before the main course that was thrust upon them, Patrick has a superb cheese soufflé and Lily’s tart of tomato with fancy cucumber and a mild cheese had incredibly delicious tomato intensity.

Back to the big people’s menu and a perfect, just cooked spear of green asparagus was simply paired with buckwheat and sorrel. With an oyster coming up and the Rougeots going down, I was thinking of Meursault still but something with a little more saline quality. A 2011 Domaine Roulot Meursault 1er Cru ‘Les Perrières’ was just what we needed. It oozes minerality. There’s so much restraint and finesse. It is compact with piercing line and all sorts of delicate citrus and peach aromas and flavours. It has great cut and superb length. The oyster in question was wrapped in a cabbage leaf and served with a mild curry sauce. There was some caramelisation to the leaf and the whole dish was remarkably tasty and textural. One of the better things I’ve eaten this year. Next, a scallop broth was delicate and intense. There were thin slices of sweet, nutty scallops mixing it up with, pumpkin, turnip, garlic chive and chicory. Seafood concluded with red mullet that became even more red with a paint brush smear of capsicum sauce down its back. Thinly sliced artichoke was cured and rolled and the whole dish had a bit of a Japanese feel to it.

Red wine was required and an aged Rousseau at a reasonable price seemed like a good proposition. The 1993 Domaine Armand Rousseau Père et Fils Ruchottes-Chambertin ‘Clos des Ruchottes’ had a complex nose of meat, blood, compost, black cherry and wild herbs. In the mouth, it had a core of sweet fruits and all sorts of meat and earth savoury nuance. It was silky with great underlying muscle and power. It will age for several more decades but boy its good now. Veal Sweetbreads were the main course and they were cooked beautifully, with some caramelisation on one side and the rest of the gland remaining light and fluffy. The sauce was perhaps the only disappointment of the day for me. It was sesame and peanut that had been reduced to an intensity that was perhaps a bit too much.

Cheese here is always good. Classic regional cheeses cannot be improved upon and we all took variations on our plates that included Reblechon, Epoisses and the like. An outstanding fruit bread and a couple of savoury conserves were served alongside fromages.

I had every intention of ordering a dessert wine but kept thinking about the 2010 Coche-Dury Puligny-Montrachet ‘Les Enseignères’ while I was pretending to look at the dessert wines on the list. The table agreed that I could order it. The nose was restrained, flinty, direct and crammed with citrus fruit. It was dense and compact, with a steely line and so much intensity. It is powerful and chalky, loaded with dry extract on the back-end. It is so not ready but so good.

Wine was pushed aside as two belting desserts hit the table. First up an egg on a nest of hay. You tap the candied shell and the yolk of cumquat oozes out. The hay is made from crème patisserie and, not only is there theatre and creativity, it is a dish that tastes fantastic. To finish, a ravioli made from mango was filled with a sweet cream. An accompanying orange was jellied and candied and still possessed tart citrus snap. Some almond soil added texture.

An excellent coffee in the bar area while the children played outside rounded off a splendid meal.

On the way back to Meursault we had a period of calm, silence while we contemplated the experience we had just had. I then asked our driver what music he liked to listen to. Colin only likes one song, and the thought of Gordon Lightfoot’s version of ‘Me and Bobby McGee’ on high rotation all the way home may have ruined the day. It was Creedence Clearwater Revival that got airplay. I think Patrick even realised what a ‘Fortunate Son’ he is after having eaten at Troisgros.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
You sense that Freddy Mugnier really likes his ‘15’s and so do we. This is indeed a brilliant vintage where there’s ripe, luscious fruit but plenty of natural tartaric acid to balance. The wines have real drive and freshness. His Amoureuses is one of the wines of the vintage for mine.

All the ‘15’s we tasted had been racked and are soon to be bottled.

2015 Jacques-Frédéric Mugnier Chambolle-Musigny: Plum and spice greet the nose. In the mouth it is fleshy with cherry fruit flavours and cherry stone minerality.

2015 Jacques-Frédéric Mugnier Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Fuées: Intense black cherry fruit is threaded with a strand of licorice. It is sweet and luscious with a lick of minerally acidity on the finish.

2015 Jacques-Frédéric Mugnier Nuits St. Georges 1er Cru Clos de la Maréchale: Ripe red and black fruits on the nose and in the mouth. It has plenty of mineral and iron and is a Nuits of finesse and class.

2015 Jacques-Frédéric Mugnier Bonnes Mares: There’s a suggestion of gingerbread on the nose. It is spicy and dark, loaded with creamy dark fruit. There’s real opulence as the luscious fruits envelop the whole palate. Length is impressive.

2015 Jacques-Frédéric Mugnier Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Amoureuses: Floral, flinty and stony. So pure and direct. There’s fleshy fruit wrapped around a high tensile spine. You get notes of sarsaparilla, pomegranate, cranberry and cherry. It is very minerally and possesses exquisite proportion and balance. A complete wine.

2015 Jacques-Frédéric Mugnier Musigny: A touch of reduction here. Very tight with deeply pitched dark fruits. It is quite stony and sappy and builds through the palate. Extremely long and possesses so much potential.

We finished with a few older bottles.

2014 Jacques-Frédéric Mugnier Nuits St. Georges 1er Cru Clos de la Maréchale: Pretty nose of smoked meats, dried flowers and cherry. It is open-knit and generous with crunchy red fruits in the mouth and plenty of savoury nuance.

2013 Jacques-Frédéric Mugnier Chambolle-Musigny: Very pretty, floral and fine. Silky of texture with stony detail. Quite perfumed and simply a lovely wine.

2008 Jacques-Frédéric Mugnier Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Fuées: Starting to take on some earthy complexity. There’s meat, floral spice and cherry too. Sweet fruit is cut by lively, mineral acid. It has intensity without weight.

posts.name: Jonathan H
posts.cooked: Love this thread! Can’t wait to try Mugnier’s 2015’s.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Since we last visited, Ghislaine Barthod has acquired Musigny, a lovely 10-month-old dog. We gave Musigny a vigorous pat and suggested she get another dog called Bonnes Mares. Later, during our tasting in the cave, we were espousing the virtues of Freddy Mugnier’s ’15 Amoureuses and Ghislaine piped up and said she was going to get a cat called Amoureuses.

Ghislaine is a beautiful person who makes beautiful wines. They are perfumed, direct stony and long-lived. She made mention that she harvested her ‘15’s around a week earlier than many of her peers.

2014 Domaine Ghislaine Barthod Chambolle-Musigny: Generous and juicy with very fresh, pure red berry fruit. Expressive now but good structure for future development.

2014 Domaine Ghislaine Barthod Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Charmes: There’s good minerality here and an engaging note of violets. Fruits are crunchy and highly perfumed and depth and structure are good.

2014 Domaine Ghislaine Barthod Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Cras: The fruit is red and black. It is fine and floral with a chalky base. There’s lovely balance and it is simply a delightful wine.

2015 Domaine Ghislaine Barthod Bourgogne: Fresh, vibrant, sweet and rich. Awesome depth for its level. Quite sappy.

2015 Domaine Ghislaine Barthod Chambolle-Musigny: Beautiful deep colour that glistens like a jewel. Luscious, sweet, black fruit overlays a mineral base. Has the depth, complexity and length of a 1er Cru wine and is an absolute buy.

2015 Domaine Ghislaine Barthod Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Aux Beaux Bruns: Generous, rich and highly perfumed. Black cherry fruits are sappy and have great flesh. It is a wine of volume and balance.

2015 Domaine Ghislaine Barthod Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Fuées: There’s more mineral here. There’s luscious cherry fruits that possess crunch. You get a touch of spice and the wine has great volume and is expansive. Superb.

2015 Domaine Ghislaine Barthod Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Véroilles: This is darker than the Fuées. Rich black cherry fruit envelop the mouth. There’s a suggestion of aniseed and licorice. It is powerful and deep with some chalk on the long finish.

2015 Domaine Ghislaine Barthod Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Cras: This has the line of Cras coupled with the generosity of the vintage. It is power with finesse. There are red and black fruits and purity and detail are awesome. It has fabulous intensity and length to burn.

A couple of beautiful bottles from Ghislaine Barthod and an excellent meal at Le Severo. The steak Hachette is killer and the côte de bœuf may be the best in Paris. 2013 Domaine Ghislaine Barthod Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Cras: Discreet, cool nose of rocks, meat, red and black fruits and violets. It is detailed and chiseled in the mouth. You feel every rocky bump and there’s enough flesh from the sweet fruit to allow you to take a peek now without getting hurt. Needs time and there’s certainly plenty of potential here.

2010 Domaine Ghislaine Barthod Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Cras: Quite an engaging nose of rose petal, smoke and black cherries. The palate has real sap and drive and a stony mineral base. It is deep and penetrating and the finish fans out. Still very youthful but thoroughly enjoyable.

I brought a '98 Barthod Les Cras to dinner last night with Merrill Lindquist, Brad Kane, Richard Trimpi (if figured they deserve boldface billing, too) and some other folks and it showed very well. A tad chunky in a '98-ish way – sort of rustic in its texture, with some tannin and a good whack of acid. Lots of beet flavors. Still very fresh and youthful, and a pleasure to drink.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Jeremy Seysses dropped in to deliver our allocation of Dujac pâté and to share a meal with us. He was on light drinking duties, following concussion from touch rugby. During our meal, his youngest son sustained an injury playing table tennis with Patrick. It seems that family Seysses are vulnerable when it comes to low impact sports.

We opened a few excellent bottles starting with a Magnum of 1986 Marc Chauvet Brut. Clotilde Chauvet had disgorged this recently for us and it was singing. The nose was complex with notes of tart tatin, mandarin, truffle, baker’s yeast and aniseed. It was rich and full, with a creamy mousse and very fine bead. There was a line of minerally acidity and length was superb. A 1973 Domaine Ramonet Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Les Ruchottes was is splendid condition, the colour was fresh and it drank beautifully. There’s still some matchstick sulphur on the nose. It had plenty of grapefruit and lemon along with spice and white mushroom. There’s excellent tension and texture and it finishes very dry with citrus rind character. A 2008 Louis Carillon Bienvenues-Bâtard-Montrachet was absolutely wonderful. There’s a lot happening on the nose…exotic fruits, vanilla, mineral and toast. The palate is rich, unctuous and layered. There’s power without weight and it has a sappy feel. The acidity draws the wine in towards the finish and then all flavours fan out. 2003 continues to pleasantly surprise me and a 2003 Domaine Dujac Morey St. Denis 1er Cru Monts-Luisants had a rich nose of ripe and heady orchard fruits, spice and honeysuckle. It is full and unctuous in the mouth. Acidity is low but it’s not overtly heavy. There’s plenty of savoury nuance and it is long. Atypical but a bloody good drink. Sadly a 1981 Clair Dau Bonnes Mares was corked. A 1990 Domaine Dujac Gevrey-Chambertin 1er Cru Aux Combottes had a fabulous nose of floral spice, smoke, sandalwood, mint and ginger. It has sweet fruit that is perfectly ripe. There’s something cool and rocky. It is lacy, ethereal, voluminous and drinking in that perfect place. We finished with a Jacques Selosse Champagne Cuvée Exquise. It was disgorged 9th of March 2016. It had complex notes of tarte tatin, green apple, peach, aniseed and honey. It is so close to being too oxidative, too sweet, too weird and too much but straddles all of those lines perfectly. Exquisite Champagne that engages the senses.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: With genuine concerns of bad frost this morning 1000’s of hay bales were distributed along the Cote yesterday. The idea is to light them before dawn and create a man made cloud to protect the sun from burning the vines too quickly if there is frost. There’s a real sense of community spirit in the air as well as a shit load of smoke. We got up early this morning to take a look. It is a sight to behold. In the lower lying areas its like a war zone with next to zero viability, burning fires and choppers flying over head. I couldn’t see any frost damage, but then again I couldn’t see much at all.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Jean-Michel and Chrstine Jacob are a couple of ripper people quietly going about their business in the hills of Echrevonne. We were quietly going about our business of making our way up to see them when the sat nav lady went quiet and neglected to tell me to get off the highway. After I realised my error we had to drive 34 km’s past our destination just to get off said highway. I noisily went about my driving, dropping a couple of cuss words along the way.

Like many along the Cote, 2016 is a year that they’d like to forget. Not because of quality, the ’16 whites and reds we have tasted have been terrific, but hail and frost hit plenty of vineyards hard. The Jacob’s have about a fifth of what they normally produce for Aligoté and Rosé and their Savigny 1er Crus will be blended to produce one small cuvee. Their ‘15’s are off the charts good. As always, the philosophy here is gentle extraction to let the fruit shine. These are wines that can be drunk with pleasure in their youth, yet they will age effortlessly for 20+ years.

2016 Domaine Lucien Jacob Bourgogne-Aligoté: Less than 100 dozen made. Compact, fresh lemon fruit pings around the gums. It is zippy, intense and utterly refreshing.

In an interesting experiment, Jean-Michel takes the same wine and presses it off into two different barrels. Both barrels are Coopered by Billon. One is made from Acacia and one from traditional oak.

2016 Domaine Lucien Jacob Bourgogne Hautes-Côtes de Beaune Les Larrets Blancs: This is the acacia barrel. There’s delicious peach and citrus fruit. It has good shape and is crisp with a tangy finish.

2016 Domaine Lucien Jacob Bourgogne Hautes-Côtes de Beaune Blanc: The traditional oak barrel wine still has the peach and citrus but is a little rounder and richer with a hint of creaminess.

2016 Domaine Lucien Jacob Savigny-lès-Beaune 1er Cru Aux Vergelesses Blanc: 4 barrels only produced. The nose is bright with crisp apple and citrus aromas. It is savoury in the mouth with plenty of mineral and a dry, chalky finish.

2016 Domaine Lucien Jacob Bourgogne Rosé: less than 100 dozen produced. Delicate and dry. There are aromas and flavours of rose petal, red fruits and mineral. It is layered in the mouth and finishes with excellent cut.

2015 Domaine Lucien Jacob Bourgogne Hautes-Côtes de Beaune: Really lovely. Cherry and berry fruits are sweet and crisp. There’s a touch of ginger spice and some supporting chalky structure. Certainly over-delivers.

2015 Domaine Lucien Jacob Savigny-lès-Beaune: It’s hard to believe this is only village wine. Perfectly ripe cherry and berry fruits are wrapped around a mineral spine. It is generous with exquisite proportion and balance.

2015 Domaine Lucien Jacob Savigny-lès-Beaune 1er Cru Les Peuillets: There’s serious concentration of black fruits here. You get some spicy sarsaparilla root and aniseed notes. Volume is good and there are earthy undertones. It builds through the palate and finishes with savoury nuance.

2015 Domaine Lucien Jacob Savigny-lès-Beaune 1er Cru Aux Vergelesses: Spicy plum and cherry fruits abound on the nose. It is full, concentrated and fleshy with great depth and a kiss of minerally acidity to the finish. There some black earth and sweet tannins and length is superb.

2015 Domaine Lucien Jacob Beaune 1er Cru Cent-Vignes: This is one of the most delicious young red Burgs I have put in my mouth. It is so sweet and supple with a sensual lusciousness. There are notes of sweet berry and violets. Waves of fleshy fruits lap around the gums. It finishes with good energy. Yummo.

2015 Domaine Lucien Jacob Beaune 1er Cru Les Toussaints: There’s a bit more grip here but the flavours are no less delicious. It is dense, dark and creamy. There’s some freshly grated ginger and a suggestion of licorice. It is complex and long.

2015 Domaine Lucien Jacob Gevrey-Chambertin: Sweet red and black fruits have a meaty, savoury edge. It has flesh, power and poise. There’s a gentle flex of muscle on the back-end. A blend of En Songe and La Justice.

2015 Domaine Lucien Jacob Chambolle-Musigny: A blend of Les Herbues, Les Athets and Aux Exchanges this is an absolute belter. It has an intoxicating aroma of violets, intense red berry and mineral. It is sweet, sappy, luscious and voluminous. It has a real juicy quality and fine structure. There’s great poise and freshness.

2013 Domaine Lucien Jacob Savigny-lès-Beaune: Drinking very nicely. The nose has sweet fruit, flora and mineral. There’s good depth of red fruit and an earthy base. It finishes with good cut.

2005 Domaine Lucien Jacob Chambolle-Musigny: Youthful, dense, dark and tight. It has good depth of cherry fruit. There’s earthy complexity and chewy tannins. It feels like it has shut down.

1995 Domaine Lucien Jacob Beaune 1er Cru Avaux: Starting to take on a bit of tertiary truffle development on the nose. There’s meat and earth too and sweet vinous fruit. It is generous and full in the mouth and cut by fresh acidity. There’s still some tannic chew on the finish.

posts.name: Scott Brunson
posts.cooked:
Thanks Jeremy.
And I think their Cassis, Framboise, and Mure are the best in the Cote.

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked: That is a great report on Jacob, Jeremy. I don’t see the whites from my source (Neal Rosenthal) and the reds are usually the Savigny-les-Beaune, Peuillets and Toussaints. I consider the Peuillets one of Burgundies great bargains with its savory, soil-driven, dark fruit. I have many customers who buy that wine by the case, and for good reason. It is about $35 here. I will watch for the Cent-Vignes and Chambolle-Musigny.

Sante, vraiment.

Mike

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
We’d requested a visit to Ponsot a couple of weeks before the announcement that Laurent was leaving the Domaine. Through the Australian importer, we heard back from Rose-Marie Ponsot that they’d like to host us for a visit and tasting. She is now running the Domaine full-time and has just appointed Alexander Abel to oversee winemaking duties. They were both generous with their time in working through a selection of their splendid 2014’s with us.

2014 Domaine Ponsot Morey St. Denis 1er Cru Clos des Monts Luisants Vieilles Vignes: Powerful and dense with great line. Loaded with citrus and a discreet floral note permeates. The finish has a salty lick.
2014 Domaine Ponsot Corton-Charlemagne: Plenty of spice on the nose along with pure orchard fruits. It has good shape in the mouth and is cut with minerally acidity.

2014 Domaine Ponsot Morey St. Denis Cuvée des Grives: Very floral perfume. There’s some freshly grated ginger and musk too. The fruits are of the red persuasion and crunchy in the mouth. It is zippy with a tart cherry snap to the finish.

2014 Domaine Ponsot Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Charmes: Deeply pitched with a core of black cherry. There’s plenty of spice on the nose and in the mouth. It is creamy and rich but has good stony detail. There’s a strong mineral impression left after swallowing.

2014 Domaine Ponsot Corton Cuvée du Bourdon: Complex aromas of sweet berry, pomegranate, root vegetables, licorice and earth. It is deep and dense with chalky tannins and a long earthy finish. It needs time.

2014 Domaine Ponsot Griotte-Chambertin: A discreet nose of cherries, violets and earth. The palate is full and rich with good intensity. It really builds and has great drive and length carried by sweet, round tannins.

2014 Domaine Ponsot Clos de la Roche Vieilles Vignes: The nose is super complex with notes of licorice, black fruits, earth, smoked meats and dried flowers. It is deep and concentrated, with sweet fruits tinged with floral spice in the mouth. It really builds and fans out on the finish. A wine of power and elegance.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Loîc Dugat gave us a warm welcome as we arrived at Dugat-Py and got straight down to the business of showing us around the Domaine. He is a proud young man and has now taken over full control of the winemaking. He did mention that Bernard can’t seem to stay away and does still help out from time to time.

The philosophy here is all about old vine fruit, power and perfume. These are wines that are built for the cellar and Loîc and Bernard recognise this by holding back stocks to release to restaurants when they have some bottle age on them. You can find some lovely ‘07’s on the lists around Burgundy at the moment.

Many of the ‘16’s are still going through malo but we were lucky enough to taste four absolute beauties. The colours on these wines from barrel are something to behold. So deep and intense but glistening like jewels.

2016 Bernard Dugat-Py Gevrey-Chambertin Vieilles Vignes: The nose is quite floral and there’s plenty of cherry stone minerality. It is dense in the moth with red and black fruits.

2016 Bernard Dugat-Py Gevrey-Chambertin Très Vieilles Vignes - Coeur de Roy: Complex nose of rose petal, pomegranate, and intense berry fruit. It is deep and stony with a dense, sweet heart. The finish is chalky.

2016 Bernard Dugat-Py Charmes-Chambertin: There’s some meaty reduction on the nose. The palate shows great purity of fruit. There’s an engaging floral thread to the wine and it is chiseled with awesome minerality. So much potential here.

2016 Bernard Dugat-Py Mazis-Chambertin: Deep, dark and brooding. It oozes minerality and is so dense and powerful in the mouth. There’s floral nuance and a finish that is direct, long and crackling with energy.

Heidi spilled a bit from her glass as we tipped back into barrel and Colin quipped ‘Crikey, you’d better give Loîc 100 euros for that. Loîc answered with ‘no, no….just 50’. He was kind enough to take us upstairs for a glass of ’07, despite the spillage.

2007 Bernard Dugat-Py Charmes-Chambertin: Complex aromatics of meat, truffle, rose petals, compost and black cherry. It has good volume and a sweet, dense heart. It is full and length is impressive. It is just starting to enter what I suspect will be a very long drinking window.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: so what’s your early take on 16 reds, jh? In the shadow of 15? Thanks and looks like a great trip!

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: I think the '16 reds are really good Alan. Yields were low to non-existent in some villages but in the main there’s excellent depth and balance. It will probably be one of those over-shadowed vintages such as '91 or '06. It is not quite as ripe, dense and luscious as '15 but there’s no shortage of ripeness.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Jean-Nicolas Méo was in fine form during our visit to Domaine Méo-Camuzet, despite carrying a serious leg injury from skiing. I guess the fact that he has a cellar full of stunning ‘15’s and ‘16’s helps blunt the pain somewhat. We chatted at length about how good the 2015’s are, and Jean-Nicolas rates it as one of the greatest vintages he has seen. We reminisced how the quality is close to being on par with the ‘99’s at a similar stage and how one particular journalist of the time, after tasting the ‘99’s, quipped ‘they’re too good to be great’. Méo’s ‘15’s are great!

2015 Domaine Méo-Camuzet Bourgogne Hautes-Côtes de Nuits Clos Saint Philibert: Full, fruity, peachy and tangy. Good volume and cut with minerally acidity.

2015 Méo-Camuzet Frère et Sœurs Fixin: There’s a strong, chalky base upon which black and red fruits play. It is vibrant and juicy and has a suggestion of licorice on the finish.

2015 Domaine Méo-Camuzet Nuits St. Georges: On the nose you get spiced plums and cherry and some smoked meats. It is complex with tart berry crunch in the mouth and cherry stone minerality. It has plenty of drive and character.

2015 Méo-Camuzet Frère et Sœurs Gevrey-Chambertin: Aromas of meat and earth and pure cherry fruit. It has some red berries on the palate and is bright and minerally with good persistence.

2015 Méo-Camuzet Frère et Sœurs Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Cras: Good punch to the aroma. Highly perfumed, floral tinged fruits leap out of the glass. There’s some grated ginger too. The palate is full, luscious and creamy with blood plum fruit and a finish that is clean and energetic.

2015 Domaine Méo-Camuzet Vosne-Romanée 1er Cru Les Chaumes: Wide open and generous with creamy dark fruits that are tinged with aniseed spice. It is velvety with outstanding volume and possesses a finish that tightens a gentle chalkiness.

2015 Domaine Méo-Camuzet Nuits St. Georges 1er Cru Les Boudots: There are some added stems here that give a delicate rose petal character. It has sweet and supple berry and cherry fruits. There’s some ginger and Chinese 5 spice powder. It is so silky against the gums and finishes with outstanding cut.

2015 Domaine Méo-Camuzet Corton-Perrières: Again some stems added here but it’s less than 10%. Complex aromatics of chalk, dark fruits, sarsaparilla root and floral spice. It is structured and powerful, carrying some puppy fat and showing plush fruits up front. The back-end is all Corton and all structure.
2015 Domaine Méo-Camuzet Corton-Clos Rognet: A little more generous than the Perrières. Black fruits are explosive and powerful. There’s plenty of Asian spice. It is muscular and meaty with a sweet core. A solid wine that is extremely dense and packed and stacked for the future.

2015 Domaine Méo-Camuzet Vosne-Romanée 1er Cru Aux Brulées: Gorgeous nose of blood plum, cherry, violets and lavender. It has so much flesh and is round, supple and sweet with a concentrated heart of perfectly ripe fruit. It is a super wine with near perfect shape and so much energy to the extremely long finish.

2015 Domaine Méo-Camuzet Vosne-Romanée 1er Cru Cros Parantoux: Cool and rocky with red and blue fruits and plenty of spice. It has remarkable presence and is chiseled but for now remains tight and shy. It builds through the palate and has wonderful underlying depth and tension with length to burn. Will be brilliant in two decades.

2016 Domaine Méo-Camuzet Vosne-Romanée 1er Cru Les Chaumes: This had just completed malo. There was some light reduction but the sweet fruit shone. It had good detail and a lacy feel in the mouth. There’s intensity without weight and it is very long.

2016 Domaine Méo-Camuzet Corton-Perrières: Also finished malo and smells of cherry and wildflowers. There’s good sap in the mouth and it oozes minerality. Balance is superb and it has so much potential.
2016 Domaine Méo-Camuzet Richebourg: Still going through malo but you feel this wine’s awesomeness. Rich, dense black fruits feel perfectly ripe. The whole mouth is enveloped with flavour. Length is supreme.

2014 Domaine Méo-Camuzet Nuits St. Georges 1er Cru Aux Murgers: A lovely nose of dried flowers, red current and cherry. It has a waxy feel in the mouth and generously fruited with flecks of mineral. Jean-Nicolas described it as ‘easy, charming and refined’. Colin said ‘just like us’.

posts.name: A1ex H
posts.cooked: Great notes on the Meo Camuzet wines - very pleased to have some 15 Brulees in the cellar.

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked:
Jeremy Holmes:

2015 Domaine Méo-Camuzet Nuits St. Georges: On the nose you get spiced plums and cherry and some smoked meats. It is complex with tart berry crunch in the mouth and cherry stone minerality. It has plenty of drive and character.

2015 Domaine Méo-Camuzet Nuits St. Georges 1er Cru Les Boudots: There are some added stems here that give a delicate rose petal character. It has sweet and supple berry and cherry fruits. There’s some ginger and Chinese 5 spice powder. It is so silky against the gums and finishes with outstanding cut.

Jean-Nicolas described it as ‘easy, charming and refined’. Colin said ‘just like us’.

Thanks, Jeremy, good stuff here as usual. As I recall, the NSG village bottling comes from a choice parcel of old vines close to or abutting Chaumes, and that Jean-Nicolas believes that it shades towards 1er cru quality. For me, it is the sleeper pick in the portfolio. Similarly, northern sector NSG 1er crus remain some of Burgundies best values and, although the Boudots above is not inexpensive (about $150-175 here recently), for me it tends to look a fair bit like the jewels from Vosne. Colin is clearly astute at self-assessment.

posts.name: J a y H a c k
posts.cooked:
Jeremy Holmes:

. . .

2012 Coche Dury Bourgogne: This was a much fresher bottle than one we had at Cagouille last week. It was bursting with sappy orchard fruits. There was some flint and good minerally structure. It had great shape and length is outstanding for its level.

I do not often dabble in this milieu, but I bought and drank a bottle of this last year when I decided that my lack of experience with Coche was a hole in my wine experience. I completely agree that for its level, it was outstanding and was bursting with fruit and minerals. I described it, perhaps incorrectly, to my wife and son as being wine that one of the greatest houses in Burgundy, though much more famous for its white wines, made from grapes that they happened to pick up from random locations of no particular note throughout the neighborhood. Even my son, who generally thinks that pinot noir is too thin and light, liked the wine.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: This was the white Jay. I have certainly enjoyed their red Bourgogne too.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Sylvie and Thomas Morey have outstanding vineyard holdings, mainly in Chassagne-Montrachet, but there’s an excellent Puligny-Montrachet and Saint-Aubin too. The cellars are undergoing major renovations but Thomas still works calmly and with great skill to turn out elegant and focused white Burgundies. His 2015 line-up show great purity of fruit. There is the richness of the vintage but each wine speaks of place and there is real clarity and fidelity. He picked relatively early and has maintained excellent freshness in each wine.

2015 Domaine Thomas Morey Bourgogne Blanc: Ripe and round orchard fruits are cut with some mineral. It is a delicious early-drinker.

2015 Domaine Thomas Morey Saint-Aubin Le Puits: Complex nose of white peach, orange zest and floral spice. It is rich, sappy and very long.

2015 Domaine Thomas Morey Chassagne-Montrachet: Smells of white flowers and white peach. It is full, round and sappy and generously fruited. There’s a lick of minerally acidity to the finish keeping everything fresh.

2015 Domaine Thomas Morey Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Les Macherelles: Open-knit and inviting with notes of spearmint, aniseed and rich, sappy orchard fruits. It builds through the palate and has a touch of saline mineral to finish.

2015 Domaine Thomas Morey Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Les Chenevottes: This is generous, full and expressive. There are whiffs of citrus blossom and ripe fruits are cut by grapefruit like acidity. It has good volume and presence.

2015 Domaine Thomas Morey Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Les Baudines: Powerful white peach fruit is tinged with floral spice. There’s some green apple too. It is full and dense, finishing quite chewy and with a puff of chalk.

2015 Domaine Thomas Morey Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Les Embrazées: White peach, citrus and spice a plenty. It is complex and really builds through the palate. There’s plenty of dry extract and the finish is very long.

2015 Domaine Thomas Morey Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Morgeot: This is classic Morgeot. Rich, ripe, round and heady. Sappy orchard fruits stain the palate and there’s an engaging thread of spice. It is intense and layered and finishes with good energy.

2015 Domaine Thomas Morey Chassagne-Montrachet Clos St. Jean: The coolness of the vineyard really suits the vintage. You get plenty of mineral coupled with ripe fruits. There’s richness but it is compact and has excellent line. There’s so much material on which to age here.

2015 Domaine Thomas Morey Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Vide Bourse: Absolutely superb. Explosive white peach fruit is cut by grapefruit acidity. It is sappy and dense with so much intensity and presence in the mouth. It has awesome length and is bright and very moreish.

2015 Domaine Thomas Morey Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Dent de Chien: A very fine nose of toast, white peach, white flowers and citrus. It is layered and textured in the mouth like Montrachet. It is full, creamy and rich yet there’s great detail and a finish that fans out.

2015 Domaine Thomas Morey Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru La Truffière: The nose is all ripe white peach and mineral with a suggestion of fennel. It is creamy, rich and round in the mouth with plenty of detail just below the flesh. It is textured and has good cut and excellent length.

2015 Domaine Thomas Morey Bâtard-Montrachet: Complex nose of orchard fruits, citrus blossom, aniseed and mineral. On the palate it is dense, powerful and unctuous without being heavy in the slightest. It has so much drive and authority and has pungent minerality on the extremely long finish.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Laurent Fournier is making some outstanding Burgundy from his Domaine in Marsannay. He has an array of high quality, old vineyards in and around Marsannay of which he farms organically. He is an intuitive maker, utilising percentages of whole bunch when he feels it necessary and having a light touch when it comes to oak. His wines are deep, pure and full of character.

2015 Domaine Jean Fournier Bourgogne: A blend off young vines within Marsannay and old vines just outside of Marsannay. This is 20% whole bunch and 100% delicious. Good depth of crunchy berry fruit. Some earth and mineral. Excellent balance and very good length for its level.

2015 Domaine Jean Fournier Marsannay Saint Urbain: 20-30-year-old vines from Clos du Roy, Es Chezots and Longerois. The wine spends 1 year in large oak and 4 months in concrete before bottling. It is a pure expression of Marsannay, driven by its delicious fruit. It is full of red and blue fruits and is luscious and creamy in the mouth. It is juicy and so drinkable now yet the depth here will ensure the wine will live for a decade or more. It is quite bright and energetic.

2015 Domaine Jean Fournier Marsannay Clos du Roy: Deep, dark and spicy. Rich, mouthfilling and sappy. Great depth and drive with sweet, round tannins carrying the long finish. Fabulous material here and a wine that should age for decades.

2015 Domaine Jean Fournier Marsannay Les Longeroies: There are red and black fruits with some floral spice notes. It is very pure and fresh in the mouth with outstanding depth of fruit. There’s a suggestion of iron and a snap of tart cherry to the finish. It really is quite lovely.

2015 Domaine Jean Fournier Marsannay Es Chezot: A very pretty nose of floral laced red berries. The fruit is concentrated in the mouth and underpinned by chalky structure. There’s a light creaminess to the wine’s texture and balance is exquisite.

2015 Domaine Jean Fournier Bourgogne Le Chapitre: Off vines planted in 1947, you feel the old vine sap to the wine. It has luscious black cherry fruit and plenty of earth. It is dense and concentrated and full of character.

2015 Domaine Jean Fournier Marsannay Trois Terres: Sadly, there’ll be no ’16 of this because of frost but the ’15 is a beauty. It has loads of spice and rich, powerful cherry fruit. It is luscious, voluminous and extremely long in the mouth.

2015 Domaine Jean Fournier Marsannay P’tite Grumotte: There’s 20% whole bunch here. It is rich, dense, spicy and full. Oak is supportive and you get good detail on the palate. For all of the wine’s power it remains light on its feet and finishes with a lick of cleansing, mineral acidity.

2015 Domaine Jean Fournier Marsannay Pur Pot: This is made from the oldest vines of Clos du Roy grown on hard limestone. There’s complex aromas of ginger, blood plum, licorice, iron and black cherry. It is a big wine with great authority and intensity. Balance and proportion are superb and it has such great persistence.

2015 Domaine Jean Fournier Bourgogne-Aligoté Champ Forey: Off two old vine parcels, 90 years old and 62 years old respectively. There are notes of candied peel, preserved lemon and iodine. It is rich, round and creamy with good depth of flavour and excellent energy.

2015 Domaine Jean Fournier Marsannay Saint Urbain Blanc: There’s 25% Pinot Blanc here. It is open-knit and expressive with aromas of honeysuckle, peach and brown spices. It is rich and textured with a refreshing skinsy feel to the finish.

2015 Domaine Jean Fournier Marsannay Clos du Roy Blanc: All white peach, honeysuckle and aniseed. It is layered in the mouth with a fruit sweet heart and great shape. There’s real persistence to the finish.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
I had met a young Chinese chap in Paris at a wine shop the week before, we were drawn to each other by the Burgundy that we were buying. He just so happened to be at Domaine Roulot when we arrived for our appointment and when introduced Heidi wondered how on earth we already knew each other. We were also introduced to David Croix who has recently joined the Domaine. He seems like a really good bloke. Jean-Marc Roulot is a really good bloke and he was eagerly awaiting the trailer for his new movie to be sent through. ‘Back to Burgundy’ is due to be released next month.

We descended to the cellar to taste the 2015’s. Bottling had been completed 10 days ago. Jean-Marc is now using Diam just for the 375ml sample bottlings. I suspected the Roulot style would suit 2015 and it sure does. The wines have so much energy and light. Jean-Marc believes the vintage to be similar to ’09 or ’12 but with a bit better acidity.

2015 Domaine Roulot Bourgogne Blanc: White peach, citrus and mineral. Picked early to maintain all of its breezy freshness. One of the best Bourgogne Blancs money can buy for mine.

2015 Domaine Roulot Meursault Les Vireuils: Quite generous and open-knit with sappy yellow fruits. The flesh is cut by minerally acidity and balance is good.

2015 Domaine Roulot Meursault Les Meix Chavaux: Intense citrus fruits on both the nose and in the mouth. There’s some candied peel flavour and good minerality. The finish has a lick of salt and a puff of chalk.

2015 Domaine Roulot Meursault Les Luchets: The aroma is shy, offering a bare whiff of citrus and blossom. In the mouth it is sappy and dense with good precision and excellent cut.

2015 Domaine Roulot Meursault Les Tillets: This is the last vintage of this wine for a while as the vineyard has been grubbed and will be re-planted. It is expressive with notes of candied citrus, white peach and almond butter. It is cut by fine, minerally acidity and has great balance and proportion.

2015 Domaine Roulot Meursault Les Tessons Clos de Mon Plaisir: Ripe orchard fruits are tinged with honeysuckle. It is full and intense with a fruit sweet heart. It has some spice and is layered and long.

2015 Domaine Roulot Meursault 1er Cru Charmes: A very rich wine with a core of white peach. There are notes of lemon, grapefruit and aniseed to. It is powerful and mouthfilling, delivering a power packed punch of flavour to every crevice in the mouth. The finish is extremely long and flecked with mineral.

2015 Domaine Roulot Meursault 1er Cru Clos des Bouchères: The is relatively tight at the moment with a delicate nose of orchard fruits and floral spice. It is intense, sappy and layered. Length is impressive.

2015 Domaine Roulot Meursault 1er Cru Les Perrières: This has the usual high tensile Perrières spine, around which intense orchard fruits gather. There’s fabulous intensity of fruit and the wine is unctuous and voluminous without being heavy in the slightest. It has great drive and presence and remarkable persistence.

We were asking Jean-Marc whether he thought there was a difference in the wine under Diam and cork, even at this early stage of the wine’s life. He sent us off to the next cellar while he set up an experiment. He opened a 2015 Luchets sealed under cork and poured us a sample of the Diam sealed and cork sealed ’15’s. They were both quite similar but Heidi and I preferred the Diam as it was a little more detailed, wearing its acidity on its sleeve. The cork sealed Luchets seemed slightly richer.

We finished with a couple of options wines.

2014 Domaine Roulot Meursault 1er Cru Charmes: Rich and expressive with pure white peach fruit. There’s a suggestion of preserved lemons and it is unctuous and powerful in the mouth. It has lovely fresh acidity and is very long.

2006 Domaine Roulot Meursault Les Luchets: Mildly exotic on the nose with some paw paw and rockmelon. It has a little honey and white mushroom development. There’s a light smokiness and the wine is full and relatively low of acidity. It is drinking right in the zone right now.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Despite a heavy travel schedule, Louis-Michel Liger-Belair was as fresh as the Spring northerly wind for our appointment. He really is an engaging chap and we conversed about travel and dining and children before settling into tasting a few ‘16’s, ‘15s and an assortment of older wines. As a set of wines, from top to bottom these are as good as anyone’s. They are sensual and fleshy but with great poise and detail. La Romanée is indeed one of the greatest vineyards on the planet. I’m certain that in a decade or two we’ll be talking about how you could once have bought Liger-Belair’s La Romanée for under $3k.

2016 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Vosne-Romanée Clos du Château: Very fine with equal parts red fruits and mineral. It has exquisite balance at this early juncture and structural elements are tucked in yet supportive.

2016 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Vosne-Romanée 1er Cru Aux Brulées: Highly perfumed with notes of violets, red berries and chalk. It is direct and crunchy with an intense, fleshy sweet heart. There’s great intensity here and it will be something special.

2016 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Nuits St. Georges 1er Cru Clos des Grandes Vignes: There’s no white version of this in ’16 with only 15kg of fruit harvested. This red is intense, sweet and concentrated. There’s plenty of spice with notes of sarsaparilla root. It finished with pomegranate-like crunch.

2016 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Vosne-Romanée 1er Cru Aux Reignots: There’s a touch of reduction but not enough to hide the wonderful floral spice complexity of this wine. It is expansive, deep, long and fresh.

2016 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Echezeaux: There’s a fine delicacy here but the wine lacks no power. It has red and black fruit and plenty of mineral. There’s an intense core of sweet cherry and it fans out on the finish like great Burgundy does.

2015 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Nuits St. Georges 1er Cru Aux Cras: A wine of great underlying structure. You get a touch of camphor to the aroma and sweet black cherry fruit. It is a big wine but balanced and there’s tart cherry snap to the finish.

2015 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Clos Vougeot: The first vintage of Clos Vougeot for Louis-Michel and an absolute beauty. Fruit is intense, dark and dense. There’s a wisp of smoke and plenty of mineral. It is extremely deep and the finish is full of cherry and cherry stone.

2015 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair La Romanée: Expansive and complex. There’s power without weight and so much going on. Fruits are black and red. There’s an engaging scent of violets and it possesses pungent minerality. It fills the whole mouth, builds right through the palate and just keeps coming at you. Near perfect wine.

2011 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Vosne-Romanée La Colombiere: The nose is loaded with floral spice. Strawberry and cherry fruits are bright and crisp and its high on the mineral on the finish.

2011 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Vosne-Romanée 1er Cru Les Suchots: Generous and open for business with its complex aromas of smoke, rose petals and sweet cherry fruit. It is luscious but there’s no shortage of detail. The finish is bright and energetic.

2010 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Echezeaux: Tight, dense, powerful and linear. There’s marvellous fruit here but its wrapped up like Tutankhamen for now. With air you get notes of aniseed and length of flavour is seriously long. Should be brilliant in a decade or two.

2010 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair La Romanée: Discreet nose with all sorts of florals and paisley fruits. It is highly perfumed and ethereal. The berry and cherry fruit has such clarity and intensity and it oozes minerality. It is cool but ripeness is perfect and proportion and balance are exquisite. The inner mouth perfume left in the mouth once swallowed is one of life’s great treats. It is so far from being ready but so good.

2007 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Vosne-Romanée 1er Cru Aux Reignots: Starting to take on some beef stock development. The nose is punchy with rose petal and pine needle sap aromas. It is fine with latent power. There’s plenty of flora, plenty of mineral and delicious red fruits. Persistence is excellent.

I definitely taste stems in the L-B wines, and I don’t believe that your statement that the wines are 100% de-stemmed is correct. There are numerous references to stem inclusion from 15% to 100% in the producer visits and tasting notes of Burghound, Tanzer and Steen Ohman. In fact Steen stated in his report of a tasting visit in 2012:

“Louis-Michel Liger-Belair is constantly working to improve quality and to refine the expression of the wines – and have made some changes over the recent years. One is more use of whole clusters in some of the wines, the other is the use of oak.”

On the other hand, the wines do see a lot of new oak, I think most cuvees rest in 100% new oak for some portion of the elevage. I’m a fan of the style of the wines, but stopped buying after 2007 because the prices in the US have really climbed into Leroy territory.

posts.name: Larry Link
posts.cooked:
Regarding the use of stems and oak, Tanzer in his 2015 report from Comte Liger-Belair states:

"Liger-Belair harvested between September 5 and 10 and the wines are all between 13% and 13.5% alcohol without chaptalization. Liger-Belair told me he did pumpovers early each morning but only two punchdowns at the beginning of the fermentations–“and that was it.” He vinified his crus with about 20% whole clusters, which he described as a bit more than usual. Some wines spent as many as 25 days in vat, also a bit longer than average for wines at this address. Other than the Vosne-Romanée Clos du Château, the 2015s were still in barrels in November and had not been sulfured since the harvest.

Incidentally, all of the wines I tasted were in 100% new barrels, which is now an exception in Burgundy. When I asked Liger-Belair about his continued preference for new oak, he responded that “even with one-year-old barrels, my wines are too reduced; there’s not enough exchange with air. Plus, the cost of a new barrel works out to only one euro per bottle.”"

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Anne-Marie and Jean-Marc Vincent are two of the great people of Burgundy. They are generous and good humoured and make splendid wines in both colours. We popped into see them late in the day for a light supper and a bit of a squiz at their marvellous ‘15’s.

Jean-Marc Vincent had recently returned from a boy’s weekend in Scotland. He assured his French buddies that they speak English in Scotland and that his English is good enough that he could translate (it is by the way). Their first encounter with a Scott had them thinking again. With a brogue as thick as chocolate mousse, a lot was lost in translation.

There’s nothing lost from vineyard to glass here, every wine speaks with complete clarity. Jean-Marc noted that the ‘15’s have a natural backbone of tartaric acid proving freshness. He also quipped that he was a little disappointed as he was unable to complain about much. He is a perfectionist.

2015 Jean-Marc Vincent Bourgogne Blanc: Picked relatively early with an alcohol of 12.5%. This is zippy wine with green melon and citrus fruits. It is cut with minerally acidity and is quite delicious.
2015 Jean-Marc Vincent Auxey-Duresses Les Hautes: Already expressive with notes of citrus blossom, white peach and preserved lemon. There’s good richness and a strong line of minerally acidity.

2015 Jean-Marc Vincent Saint-Aubin 1er Cru: This is off Sur Gamay and La Combe. It is fresh and stony with plenty of citrus. There’s generosity in the mouth and it tightens towards the end finishing with a puff of chalk.

2015 Jean-Marc Vincent Santenay 1er Cru Beaurepaire Blanc: Tight and dense. There are yellow fruits and plenty of saline minerality. It has great shape and line and is so fresh and direct.

2015 Jean-Marc Vincent Santenay Vieilles Vignes: Plenty of whole bunch punch from the inclusion of 60% stems. There are sweet berry and cherry fruits and the scent of rose petals. It is rich yet elegant and it is deliciously sappy against the gums. It finishes with mineral freshness.

2015 Jean-Marc Vincent Santenay 1er Cru Beaurepaire Rouge: Exquisite cherry fruit is laced with ginger spice. It is luscious and round with great depth and excellent length.

2015 Jean-Marc Vincent Santenay 1er Cru Gravières: Complex aromatics of pomegranate, cherry, aniseed and rose petal. It is round and rich but has outstanding underlying tension and detail. There’s awesome drive and it is simply a wonderful and complete red Burgundy.

2015 Jean-Marc Vincent Santenay 1er Cru Passetemps: Dense and deep, very much black fruited. There’s a chalky base upon which luscious, floral laden fruit plays. Tannins are sweet and it drives on and on, fanning out on the finish.
2015 Jean-Marc Vincent Auxey-Duresses 1er Cru Breterins: Stony and earthy with red and black fruits. It has flesh and generosity but structural elements add good grip. The finish is very minerally.

2014 Jean-Marc Vincent Santenay Gravité: This wine possesses immaculate posture thanks to its strong mineral line. Fruits are perfectly ripe and floral tinged. There’s great interplay between sweet and savoury elements and intensity and length are super.

2012 Jean-Marc Vincent Santenay Gravité: Still primary, fresh and very youthful. It is round and dense with sweet dark fruits. It has good volume and power and flavours are carried by a line of mienrally acid and sinewy tannins.

2007 Jean-Marc Vincent Santenay 1er Cru Beaurepaire Blanc: Taking on some toast and truffle development. Good shape in the mouth with a fruit sweet heart and grape fruit cut to the finish.

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked: Great report here, Jeremy, thank you. This is a domaine that should be on the radar of all, but particularly young Burgundy buyers on a budget. The 1er crus are about $50 here in CA and I can’t think of more wine for the money in either color. I expected that the Vincents would make lovely reds in 2015, as usual, and that their whites might have some flesh to add a horizontal aspect to their otherwise laser-like character. It sounds like that might be the case. I grabbed a bottle of the Les Hautes when leaving my warehouse today to have a look for myself.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
We were the only ones to turn up at the designated time for our appointment at Dujac with Jeremy Seysses. A team of South Africans had phoned through to advise they were still at lunch, some Americans turned up a month early and some other Americans just turned up. Jeremy split us into two cellars and was in and out like a fiddler’s elbow but conducted our tasting with great alacrity.

His ‘15’s are off the charts good. There’s a fairly high proportion of whole bunch in each wine (around 90%).

2015 Domaine Dujac Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru Les Folatières: The nose is all candied peel and ripe peach. It is full, sappy and round in the mouth and fresh acidity keeps everything in check.

2015 Domaine Dujac Chambolle-Musigny: Exquisite cherry fruit has that just dusted with icing sugar sweetness about it. There are notes of rose petal too. It is very pretty and already expressive, building through the palate and finishing with good cut.

2015 Domaine Dujac Morey St. Denis: Sweet red, blue and black fruits are tinged with floral spice. It is rich and creamy with great volume for its level. The finish is fresh and it is an utterly delicious wine.

2015 Domaine Dujac Gevrey-Chambertin 1er Cru Aux Combottes: Very tight now and Jeremy mentioned that it is tighter than Borat’s sister (not me, the other rude Jeremy). There’s intense raspberry and cherry fruit overlaying chalky structure. The finish has excellent grip.

2015 Domaine Dujac Charmes-Chambertin,Grand Cru: Inviting, complex and open. The fruit is black and sweet. There’s plenty of earth and it is juicy and round in the mouth. The finish has a suggestion of mineral and good freshness to it.

2015 Domaine Dujac Clos de la Roche,Grand Cru: Complex, deep and powerful. It has notes of licorice, dark cherry and sweet earth. It is concentrated and explosive with great volume. The floral whole bunch things marry well with all of the ripe fruits and sweet tannins carry the wine for an eternity.

2015 Domaine Dujac Clos St. Denis,Grand Cru: This is an intense wine with the perfect balance between luscious fruit and cool stony things. It is fleshy with great power and plenty of muscle on the back-end. Length is superb.

2015 Domaine Dujac Vosne-Romanée 1er Cru Aux Malconsorts: Very pretty nose of Chinese 5 spice, dried flowers and plum and cherry fruits. It is luscious, velvety and voluminous in the mouth. There’s a gentle clench of tannin towards the finish and over-all harmony and poise is terrific.

2015 Domaine Dujac Echezeaux,Grand Cru: Loaded with blackberry, cherry and raspberry fruits and laced with an intoxicating floral spice. The palate is rich and full but possesses excellent detail. The floral spice carries on right through to the finish and it has really good finesse and persistence. A wonderful Echezeaux.

We finished with a couple of cheeky half bottles.

2006 Domaine Dujac Clos St. Denis,Grand Cru: Sweet berry fruit is trimmed with earth. It has some stemsy lift and good shape in the mouth. Tannins are refined and length very good.

1999 Domaine Dujac Clos de la Roche,Grand Cru: A really punchy nose of dried flowers, soy, smoke and sandalwood. The mid-palate is fabulously rich and sweet and it is a wine that has serious depth and build. Still just a baby but just starting to unfurl.

posts.name: Howard Davis
posts.cooked:
Jeremy, thanks for your notes.

It reminds me of my visit last year, where we tasted '15s in barrel and '14s in bottle. That '15s Malconsorts, from my brief taste, seemed a superb wine. Jeremy said it had (at that stage) always been a star of 2015. We were also lucky to have a little vertical of CdlR, the '15, '14 and '99. The '99 was, as you describe it, a beauty.

BTW, I’m genuinely shocked that people who are lucky enough to secure an appointment at a house like Dujac, treat it the way you describe. I suppose at least the lunch people phoned through their apologies …
